question: [File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: _mintToken / _burnToken] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is the token beneficiary of a TOKEN_V3_PERMIT token under PRECONDITIONS where the token has accumulated bonding-curve fees (DEFAULT_V4_NON_TAX_FEE_RATE_BPS = 125 bps sent to beneficiary on every buy/sell) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [newTokenV7 with TOKEN_V3_PERMIT → multiple swapExactInput buys/sells → claim], violating INVARIANT that total BNB paid out via BeneficiaryClaimed must not exceed sum of all fee amounts recorded in TokenBought/TokenSold events for that token, causing scoped impact: attacker-controlled beneficiary overclaims bonding-curve fee proceeds beyond valid entitlement? Local proof idea: add fork-test on BSC block 108382650 with PARAMETERS [TOKEN_V3_PERMIT token, 10 buy/sell cycles, claim after each cycle] and assert sum(BeneficiaryClaimed.ethAmount) == sum(TokenBought.fee + TokenSold.fee).

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: _burnToken (payer == address(this) branch)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is a seller under PRECONDITIONS where a TOKEN_V2_PERMIT or TOKEN_V3_PERMIT token has a fee-on-transfer hook that retains a portion of every transfer trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [swapExactInput sell → safeTransferFrom(payer, address(this), amount) receives amount-fee → _burnToken skips re-transfer and decrements circulatingSupply by full amount], violating INVARIANT that Portal's actual ERC20 balance of the token must equal maxSupply - circulatingSupply at all times, causing scoped impact: Portal's token balance drifts below maxSupply - circulatingSupply, enabling a later buyer to receive tokens that Portal cannot actually deliver, effectively extracting value from other users' reserves? Local proof idea: add unit test with a mock ERC20 that takes 1% fee on transfer, run 100 sell cycles, assert portal.balanceOf(token) == maxSupply - circulatingSupply after each cycle.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: _eraseToken] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where _eraseToken is called (e.g., during a migration or launch path) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [newTokenV7 → migration path calls _eraseToken(token, X) → circulatingSupply is NOT decremented → subsequent _mintToken(token, Y, recipient) transfers tokens Portal no longer holds], violating INVARIANT that Portal's ERC20 balance of any V2+ token must always be >= maxSupply - circulatingSupply, causing scoped impact: Portal cannot fulfill a legitimate buyer's token purchase, or a buyer receives fewer tokens than circulatingSupply accounting implies, creating an insolvency that benefits an attacker who sells before the shortfall is discovered? Local proof idea: add invariant fuzz test asserting portal.balanceOf(token) >= maxSupply - state.circulatingSupply after every _eraseToken call.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: _getTokenState (legacy header branch, lines 858-915)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a legacy PackedTokenStateLegacy token has its status byte set to 0xff (PACKED_TOKEN_STATE_HEADER = 0xff) through any reachable path trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [_setTokenStatus(token, TokenStatus(0xff)) → _getTokenState misparses slot0 as PackedTokenStateV2 → reads reserve from slot1 instead of bits 56-151 of slot0 → reads circulatingSupply from slot1 upper 128 bits instead of bits 152-247 of slot0], violating INVARIANT that _getTokenState must return the same reserve and circulatingSupply values that were written by _setTokenReserve and _setTokenCirculatingSupply, causing scoped impact: attacker exploits the misparse to buy tokens at a stale/wrong price derived from corrupted reserve/supply, extracting BNB from the bonding curve? Local proof idea: add differential unit test comparing _getTokenState output before and after a status byte collision for a legacy token.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: _setTokenReserve (legacy branch, line 960)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a legacy token's reserve grows beyond uint96.max (2^96 - 1 ≈ 7.92e28 wei) through accumulated buys trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [many swapExactInput buys → reserve accumulates → _setTokenReserve stores uint96(reserve) truncating upper bits → _getTokenState reads back truncated value → bonding curve price computed from wrong reserve], violating INVARIANT that estimateReserve(circulatingSupply) must equal the actual BNB held by Portal for that token, causing scoped impact: attacker sells tokens and receives more BNB than the bonding curve formula entitles them to, draining the reserve? Local proof idea: add fuzz test with reserve values near uint96.max and assert _getTokenState().reserve == actual_reserve_written.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: _circulatingSupply (legacy branch, line 729)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a legacy TOKEN_LEGACY_MINT_NO_PERMIT token's IToken.totalSupply() can be inflated externally (e.g., the legacy token contract has a public mint function callable by anyone) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [attacker calls token.mint(attacker, large_amount) directly → _circulatingSupply returns inflated totalSupply → bonding curve computes wrong price → attacker sells at inflated price], violating INVARIANT that circulatingSupply used in bonding curve calculations must equal tokens actually distributed through Portal's buy paths, causing scoped impact: attacker extracts excess BNB from the bonding curve reserve by selling at a price computed from an inflated supply? Local proof idea: add fork-test checking whether any live legacy token's IToken implementation has an unrestricted mint function callable by non-Portal addresses.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: claim (dispatched to PORTAL_ROLLER)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is the current beneficiary of a token under PRECONDITIONS where setTokenBeneficiary has been called to change the beneficiary to attacker-controlled address trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [setTokenBeneficiary(token, attacker) → LP fees accumulate in lpLocks → attacker calls claim(token) → BeneficiaryClaimed emitted with attacker as beneficiary → attacker receives all accumulated LP fees], violating INVARIANT that only the original token creator or a legitimately authorized beneficiary should receive LP fee proceeds, causing scoped impact: attacker redirects all accumulated LP fee rewards away from the legitimate beneficiary? Local proof idea: add unit test asserting that setTokenBeneficiary access control prevents unprivileged callers from changing _tokenBeneficiaries[token].

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: delegateClaim (dispatched to PORTAL_ROLLER)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who holds ROLLER_ROLE under PRECONDITIONS where _tokenBeneficiaries[token] has been set to an attacker-controlled address trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [attacker with ROLLER_ROLE calls delegateClaim(token) → fees sent to _tokenBeneficiaries[token] = attacker → BeneficiaryClaimed emitted], violating INVARIANT that delegateClaim must only forward fees to the legitimately registered beneficiary and not allow a roller to redirect fees to an attacker-controlled address via a prior setTokenBeneficiary call, causing scoped impact: attacker extracts all LP fee rewards from a token they do not own? Local proof idea: add integration test verifying that delegateClaim sends fees to the address in _tokenBeneficiaries[token] and that _tokenBeneficiaries cannot be set by an unprivileged caller.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: collectV4Fees (dispatched to PORTAL_V4_CL_LOCKER)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a TOKEN_TAXED_V3 token has accumulated V4/PCS Infinity LP fees and the TaxProcessor's fee distribution logic sends a portion to the token beneficiary trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [attacker calls collectV4Fees(token) → fees flow Portal → TaxProcessor → distribution → portion sent to _tokenBeneficiaries[token]], violating INVARIANT that collectV4Fees must not allow an unprivileged caller to trigger fee distribution that results in more value flowing to the beneficiary than the LP position actually earned, causing scoped impact: attacker front-runs or manipulates the fee collection timing to extract LP fee rewards beyond valid entitlement? Local proof idea: add fork-test calling collectV4Fees multiple times in the same block and asserting V4LPFeesCollected.quoteAmount is consistent with actual pool fee accrual.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: addV4LPLiquidity (dispatched to PORTAL_V4_CL_LOCKER)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a TOKEN_TAXED_V3 token's TaxProcessor calls addV4LPLiquidity and Portal holds token/quote balances from a prior collectV4Fees trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [collectV4Fees → TaxProcessor calls addV4LPLiquidity(token, tokenAmount, quoteAmount) → Portal transfers tokens/quote to LP positions → attacker sandwiches the LP addition to extract value from the price impact], violating INVARIANT that addV4LPLiquidity must not allow an unprivileged caller to trigger LP additions that result in value leakage from Portal's held balances, causing scoped impact: attacker extracts BNB or quote tokens from Portal's balance through sandwich attacks on the LP addition? Local proof idea: add fork-test simulating a sandwich attack around addV4LPLiquidity and measuring Portal's net BNB balance change.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: _mintToken (V2+ branch, lines 756-773)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a TOKEN_V2_PERMIT or TOKEN_V3_PERMIT token is being bought and the recipient is a contract with an onERC20Received callback trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [swapExactInput buy → _mintToken calls safeTransfer(recipient, amount) → recipient's callback calls swapExactInput sell → _burnToken(token, amount, address(this)) decrements circulatingSupply → outer _mintToken then increments circulatingSupply again], violating INVARIANT that circulatingSupply after a buy-then-sell reentrancy must equal circulatingSupply before the buy, causing scoped impact: attacker exploits the reentrancy to corrupt circulatingSupply accounting, enabling future buys at a wrong price that extracts excess BNB from the reserve? Local proof idea: add reentrancy unit test with a mock ERC20 receiver that sells back immediately and assert circulatingSupply is consistent before and after.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: _getTokenState (slot2 optimization, lines 897-905)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a token was created with a non-native ERC20 quote token but the quoteToken field in PackedTokenStateV2 is corrupted to QuoteTokenType.NATIVE_GAS_TOKEN (e.g., through a storage collision or upgrade bug) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [swapExactInput buy with ERC20 quote → _getTokenState skips slot2 read → state.quoteTokenAddress = address(0) → trade logic treats it as native BNB → attacker pays BNB instead of ERC20 quote token], violating INVARIANT that the quote token used in trade accounting must match the token's registered quoteTokenAddress, causing scoped impact: attacker buys tokens by paying BNB when the reserve is denominated in a more valuable ERC20, extracting value from the quote token reserve? Local proof idea: add storage-manipulation unit test setting quoteToken field to NATIVE_GAS_TOKEN for an ERC20-quote token and asserting swapExactInput reverts or uses correct quote token.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupplyV2 / estimateReserveV2 (Curve.sol lines 101-157)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a token uses a non-18-decimal ERC20 quote token (e.g., USDC with 6 decimals) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [swapExactInput buy with USDC → estimateSupplyV2 scales reserve by 10^12 → divWadUp rounds down supply → attacker receives more tokens than the curve formula entitles → swapExactInput sell → estimateReserveV2 scales back with ceiling division → attacker receives more USDC than they paid], violating INVARIANT that buy(amount_in) followed by sell(tokens_received) must return <= amount_in in quote tokens, causing scoped impact: attacker extracts USDC from the bonding curve reserve through repeated buy-sell cycles exploiting decimal scaling rounding? Local proof idea: add fuzz test with USDC (6 decimals) buy-sell round trips asserting net_quote_out <= net_quote_in for all input amounts.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupply / estimateReserve (Curve.sol lines 47-67)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a token uses a legacy curve (h=0, k=r*TOTAL_SUPPLY) and the reserve is near the DEX threshold trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [buy just below DEX threshold → estimateSupply uses divWadUp rounding → attacker receives slightly more tokens than formula → circulatingSupply crosses dexSupplyThresh → migration triggered → attacker's excess tokens are now DEX tokens worth more], violating INVARIANT that estimateSupply(reserve) must not return a supply value that exceeds what the constant-product formula allows, causing scoped impact: attacker extracts excess tokens at the DEX graduation boundary through rounding exploitation? Local proof idea: add differential fuzz test comparing estimateSupply output against a high-precision reference implementation near the DEX threshold.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: _setTokenCirculatingSupply (legacy branch, lines 991-1006)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a legacy token's circulatingSupply is written as uint96 (bits 152-247 of slot0) but read back as uint128(uint96(...)) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [_mintToken for legacy token → IToken.totalSupply() returns value > uint96.max → _setTokenCirculatingSupply stores truncated uint96 → _getTokenState reads truncated value → bonding curve computes price from wrong supply], violating INVARIANT that _getTokenState().circulatingSupply must equal the actual number of tokens in circulation, causing scoped impact: attacker exploits the truncated supply to buy tokens at a price computed from an artificially low supply, extracting excess BNB from the reserve? Local proof idea: add unit test with a mock legacy token whose totalSupply() returns uint96.max + 1 and assert _getTokenState().circulatingSupply matches the actual value.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: lockSalt / _saltLocks] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where SALT_LOCK_FEE > 0 and the attacker has locked a salt for TOKEN_V3_PERMIT trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [lockSalt(salt, TOKEN_V3_PERMIT) paying SALT_LOCK_FEE → _saltLocks[salt] = {locker: attacker, tokenVersion: TOKEN_V3_PERMIT, isUsed: false} → attacker calls newTokenV7 with same salt and TOKEN_TAXED_V3 → SaltLockTokenVersionMismatch revert → attacker calls newTokenV7 with TOKEN_V3_PERMIT → FlapSaltUsed emitted → attacker receives token with vanity address], violating INVARIANT that the SALT_LOCK_FEE paid by the locker must not be refundable or reusable after the salt is consumed, causing scoped impact: attacker locks a salt, uses it for a launch, and then attempts to claim a refund or reuse the fee payment for another salt lock? Local proof idea: add unit test verifying that after FlapSaltUsed, the same salt cannot be re-locked and the fee is non-refundable.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: lockSalt / VAULT_PORTAL / ISaltOwnerProvider.getSaltOwner] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where VAULT_PORTAL is set to a non-zero address trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [attacker deploys a contract that implements ISaltOwnerProvider.getSaltOwner() returning victim's address → attacker calls lockSalt from their contract (not VAULT_PORTAL) → Portal does NOT call getSaltOwner() since msg.sender != VAULT_PORTAL → attacker locks salt under their own address → attacker launches token using victim's intended salt], violating INVARIANT that salt ownership must be correctly attributed to the actual payer and not be bypassable through the VAULT_PORTAL callback path, causing scoped impact: attacker steals a victim's pre-planned vanity token address by locking the salt first, capturing the token-launch related reward (vanity address + first-buyer advantage)? Local proof idea: add unit test verifying that lockSalt correctly attributes salt ownership to msg.sender when msg.sender != VAULT_PORTAL.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: _userSaltsByVersion / getSaltLock] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a salt was locked before v5.11.0 (pre-upgrade lock not in _userSaltsByVersion) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [attacker queries getLockedSaltsCountByUserAndVersion(victim, TOKEN_V3_PERMIT) → returns 0 (pre-upgrade lock not indexed) → attacker assumes salt is unlocked → attacker calls lockSalt(salt, TOKEN_V3_PERMIT) → SaltAlreadyLockedByAnotherUser revert], violating INVARIANT that the on-chain index must not create a false impression of salt availability that leads to fee loss, causing scoped impact: attacker pays SALT_LOCK_FEE and loses it due to the index gap, or alternatively, the index gap allows an attacker to front-run a victim's launch by locking a salt the victim holds pre-upgrade? Local proof idea: add fork-test on BSC checking whether any pre-v5.11.0 salt locks exist that are not reflected in _userSaltsByVersion.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: _tokenBeneficiaries / setTokenBeneficiary] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is the token creator under PRECONDITIONS where the token was created with beneficiary = address(0) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [newTokenV7 with beneficiary = address(0) → _tokenBeneficiaries[token] = address(0) → LP fees accumulate → attacker calls setTokenBeneficiary(token, attacker) → attacker calls claim(token) → all accumulated fees sent to attacker], violating INVARIANT that a zero beneficiary address must not be claimable by any unprivileged caller, causing scoped impact: attacker captures all accumulated LP fee rewards for a token that was launched without a beneficiary? Local proof idea: add unit test verifying that setTokenBeneficiary access control prevents an unprivileged caller from setting themselves as beneficiary for a token with address(0) beneficiary.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: FlapFeeProfile.FEE_ZERO / _buyFeeByProfile / _sellFeeByProfile] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a token has been assigned FEE_ZERO profile (via setFlapFeeProfile by TOKEN_FLAP_FEE_SETTER_ROLE) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [setFlapFeeProfile(token, FEE_ZERO) → _buyFeeByProfile returns 0 → _sellFeeByProfile returns 0 → attacker buys and sells with zero protocol fee → but DEFAULT_V4_NON_TAX_FEE_RATE_BPS = 125 bps bonding-curve fee still applies for TOKEN_V3_PERMIT → fee goes to beneficiary], violating INVARIANT that FEE_ZERO profile must zero out ALL fees including the bonding-curve fee sent to the beneficiary, or alternatively that FEE_ZERO must not zero out the bonding-curve fee while leaving the beneficiary fee intact, causing scoped impact: attacker who is the beneficiary collects 1.25% bonding-curve fees from all trades while paying zero protocol fee, extracting disproportionate rewards? Local proof idea: add unit test verifying the interaction between FEE_ZERO profile and DEFAULT_V4_NON_TAX_FEE_RATE_BPS for TOKEN_V3_PERMIT tokens.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: TAX_PROC_BONDING_CURVE_FEE_RATE / TAX_PROC_DEX_FEE_RATE] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where a TOKEN_TAXED_V3 token is near the DEX graduation threshold trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [attacker buys enough tokens to push circulatingSupply just below dexSupplyThresh → migration triggered → TAX_PROC_DEX_FEE_RATE = 4100 (41%) replaces TAX_PROC_BONDING_CURVE_FEE_RATE = 6000 (60%) → attacker sells post-migration paying lower protocol fee → attacker captures the 19% fee rate difference as excess reward], violating INVARIANT that the fee rate transition from bonding curve (60%) to DEX (41%) must not create an exploitable window where an attacker can extract value by timing trades around the migration, causing scoped impact: attacker extracts excess tax rewards by front-running the DEX graduation fee rate change? Local proof idea: add fork-test simulating a buy that triggers migration and measuring the fee rate applied to the first post-migration sell.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: updateTaxTokenAddresses (dispatched to PORTAL_TWEAK)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where the attacker holds TAX_MANAGER_ROLE or TAX_GUARDIAN_ROLE trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [updateTaxTokenAddresses([{token: victim_tax_token, beneficiary: attacker, feeReceiver: attacker}]) → TaxTokenAddressesUpdated emitted → all future tax distributions for victim_tax_token go to attacker], violating INVARIANT that tax token beneficiary and feeReceiver addresses must only be changeable by fully privileged admin roles and not by TAX_MANAGER_ROLE or TAX_GUARDIAN_ROLE acting as unprivileged attackers, causing scoped impact: attacker with a lower-privilege role redirects all tax rewards from a victim token to their own address? Local proof idea: add unit test verifying the exact role requirements for updateTaxTokenAddresses and whether TAX_MANAGER_ROLE alone is sufficient to redirect beneficiary.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: changeMarketWallet (dispatched to PORTAL_TWEAK)] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS where the attacker holds TAX_GUARDIAN_ROLE trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [changeMarketWallet(token, attacker_address) → MarketWalletChanged emitted → TaxProcessor.marketAddress = attacker → all future mktBps tax distributions for that token go to attacker], violating INVARIANT that the market wallet (which receives mktBps of all tax collected) must not be redirectable by a TAX_GUARDIAN_ROLE holder acting as an unprivileged attacker, causing scoped impact: attacker with TAX_GUARDIAN_ROLE captures all ongoing marketing/vault tax rewards from a victim token? Local proof idea: add unit test verifying that changeMarketWallet with TAX_GUARDIAN_ROLE successfully redirects tax distributions and measuring the financial impact.

[File: 'src/PortalBase.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: _mintToken / _burnToken] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is the token beneficiary of a TOKEN_V3_PERMIT token under PRECONDITIONS where the token has accumulated bonding-curve fees (DEFAULT_V4_NON_TAX_FEE_RATE_BPS = 125 bps sent to beneficiary on every buy/sell) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE [newTokenV7 with TOKEN_V3_PERMIT → multiple swapExactInput buys/sells → claim], violating INVARIANT that total BNB paid out via BeneficiaryClaimed must not exceed sum of all fee amounts recorded in TokenBought/TokenSold events for that token, causing scoped impact: attacker-controlled beneficiary overclaims bonding-curve fee proceeds beyond valid entitlement?
